section: High-strength Fe–20Mn–Al–C-based Alloys with Low Density

context_update.summary: This chunk introduces the Fe–20Mn–Al–C and Fe–20Mn–Al–C–5Cr alloy systems with compositional ranges: Al 10-14 mass%, C 0-1.8 mass%, with or without 5 mass% Cr. Alloys were hot-rolled at 1200°C to about 1.5 mm sheets, then heat treated at 900–1100°C for 15 min, followed by water quenching or air cooling. A simple density estimation method for fcc alloys was proposed, using elemental density parameters D_Fe=8.19, D_Mn=7.19, D_Al=2.70, D_Cr=6.36, D_C=2.85 g/cm³. Figure 1 presents iso-density contours on the Fe–20Mn–Al–C phase diagram at 1100°C, showing γ single-phase alloys can achieve density below 7.0 g/cm³. Key findings include: γ alloys with density <7.0 g/cm³ show excellent ductility; hardness and tensile strength increase with Al and C due to nano-size κ-carbide precipitation; a Fe–20Mn–11Al–1.8C–5Cr alloy reached density 6.51 g/cm³, specific strength >180 MPa·cm³/g, elongation 40%, outperforming conventional steels. The cold-workability parameter W was defined. Subsequent chunks will likely present hardness, tensile, cold-workability results, and microstructural evidence.
processing_route: hot rolling at 1200°C to ~1.5 mm sheets
heat treatment at 900–1100°C for 15 min + water quenching or air cooling
key_variables: cooling rate (water quench vs air cool)
properties: hardness and tensile strength increase with Al and C
mechanisms: precipitation hardening by nano-size κ-carbide during cooling from annealing temperature
figures_or_tables: Figure 1: phase diagram with iso-density lines at 1100°C

context_update.summary: This chunk validates the density estimation method and presents mechanical properties of water-quenched Fe–20Mn–Al–C and Fe–20Mn–Al–C–5Cr alloys. The iso-density contours on the phase diagram (Figure 1) confirm that γ single-phase alloys can achieve density below 7.0 g/cm³, and the formula extends to γ+α alloys with acceptable accuracy. Hardness and cold-workability maps (Figure 2) reveal composition–phase–property correlations; Cr shifts phase boundaries, suppresses κ-carbide, enabling higher Al/C γ alloys with both high hardness and good cold-workability. Optical micrographs (Figure 3) identify γ, γ+κ, and γ+α phases. Quantitative tensile data (Table 1, Figure 4) show specific strength >180 MPa·cm³/g and ~30% elongation for Fe–20Mn–11Al–1.8C–5Cr, attributed to fine κ precipitation during quenching. The segment also introduces cooling rate as a variable for subsequent study on the Fe–20Mn–10Al–1.5C–5Cr alloy. Future chunks should track aging/cooling rate effects and further κ-carbide characterization.
key_variables: Cooling rate (water quench; investigation of cooling rate effect ongoing)
microstructure: γ+κ phase mixture (κ-carbide Fe₃AlC with perovskite structure)
κ-carbide suppression by Cr in quinary alloys
Very fine κ precipitates formed during quenching from >900°C
properties: Hardness (Hv): up to 500 Hv in quinary γ+α alloys
Cold-workability: 0% for brittle κ-containing alloys, >90% for some γ alloys
Tensile yield strength up to 1240 MPa (Table 1)
Specific strength exceeding 180 MPa·cm³/g for Fe–20Mn–11Al–1.8C–5Cr
Upper yield point and constant flow stress in certain γ alloys
mechanisms: C content increase reduces α volume fraction, improving ductility
Cr addition shifts γ/γ+κ boundary to higher C, suppressing κ formation and improving cold-workability
Fine κ precipitation during water quenching strengthens γ alloys without large ductility loss
Stable κ phase at annealing temperature leads to brittleness

section: 4.1. Compositional Dependence of Mechanical Properties in Water-quenched Alloys

The iso-density contours on the phase diagram (Figure 1) confirm that γ single-phase alloys can achieve density below 7.0 g/cm³, and the formula extends to γ+α alloys with acceptable accuracy. context_update.summary: This chunk completes the compositional dependence section (4.1) for water-quenched alloys, then addresses cooling rate effects (4.2) on Fe–20Mn–10Al–1.5C–5Cr γ alloy, and precipitation/work hardening (4.3). Key findings: Fe–20Mn–11Al–1.8C–5Cr achieves specific strength >180 MPa·cm³/g and ~30% elongation due to nano κ precipitation during quenching; air cooling (~15°C/s) produces much higher yield strength, upper yield point, and nearly zero initial work‑hardening compared to water quenching (~1100°C/s), linked to dense cuboidal κ aligned along <001> (Fig. 6). True stress‑strain curves, diffraction patterns, and dark‑field images quantify these differences. Air‑cooled and cold‑rolled properties are tabulated (Tables 2–4), highlighting specific strengths up to 200 MPa·cm³/g for γ+α, 230 MPa·cm³/g after 30% cold rolling, and >300 MPa·cm³/g at >90% reduction albeit with limited ductility. The spinodal decomposition hypothesis for κ formation is noted. Figure 8 places these alloys against conventional steels. Subsequent chunks should track κ‑carbide evolution, the role of Cr‑carbide noted at lower annealing temperatures, and further mechanistic understanding.
microstructure: very fine κ precipitates in WQ
cuboidal κ precipitates aligned <001> in AC
Cr-carbide phases at lower annealing temperature
mechanisms: nano κ precipitation hardening during cooling
possible spinodal decomposition for κ formation
unresolved_terms: formation mechanism of very fine κ precipitates during cooling not clear

context_update.summary: This chunk covers Section 4.4 and the conclusions. Figure 8 compares specific strength vs elongation across steels: the present Fe–Mn–Al–C(–Cr) alloys show higher specific strength than conventional and TWIP steels, and are simpler to process than TRIP steels. The conclusions summarize: γ alloy strength rises with Al and C, but elongation falls; γ+α alloys can balance strength and ductility via α fraction control. Cr shifts the γ/γ+κ boundary to higher C and widens the γ+α region to higher Al. Air‑cooled γ alloy (Fe–20Mn–10Al–1.5C–5Cr) develops fine cuboidal κ, yielding higher hardness, strength, and an upper yield point relative to water quenching. The Fe–20Mn–11Al–1.8C–5Cr alloy achieves specific strength >180 MPa·cm³/g and 40% elongation at density 6.51 g/cm³. Cold rolling above 90% reduction gives >300 MPa·cm³/g. Following chunks should retain these final property benchmarks, the role of κ precipitation, Cr’s effect on phase boundaries, and the process simplicity advantage.

processing_route: cooling rate control (air cooling vs water quenching)
microstructure: suppression of κ carbide by Cr

phase boundary shift by Cr addition
process simplicity advantage (annealing and cooling rate control)
